Previously, the heads of families were priests and offered sacrifices, but now this office was restricted to Aaron’s family only; it continued like this until the gospel age. The holy garments not only distinguished the priests from the people, but they were symbols of that holy conduct which should always be the glory and beauty, the mark of religious leaders. Without this, their persons and ministrations will be viewed with contempt. They also symbolized the splendor of God’s majesty and the beauty of complete holiness, which made Jesus Christ the great High Priest. But our adornment under the gospel is not to be of gold and expensive clothing, but the garments of salvation, the robe of righteousness.

This richly-wrought ephod was the outermost garment of the high priest; plain linen ephods were worn by lesser priests. It was a short coat without sleeves, fastened close to the body with a girdle. The shoulder pieces were buttoned together with precious stones set in gold, one on each shoulder, which had the names of the children of Israel engraved upon them. Thus Christ, our High Priest, presents his people before the Lord as a memorial. Just as Christ’s coat had no seam but was woven from top to bottom, so it was with the ephod. The golden bells on this ephod, by their preciousness and pleasant sound, well represent the good profession that the saints make, and the pomegranates symbolize the fruit they bring forth.

The chief ornament of the high priest was the breastplate, a rich piece of cloth, intricately worked. The name of each tribe was engraved on a precious stone, fixed in the breastplate, to signify how precious believers are in God’s sight and how honorable. No matter how small or poor a tribe was, it was as a precious stone in the breastplate of the high priest; thus all the saints are dear to Christ, no matter how people esteem them. The high priest had the names of the tribes both on his shoulders and on his chest, which reminds us of the power and love with which our Lord Jesus pleads for those who are his. He not only supports them with almighty strength but carries them in his bosom with tender affection. What comfort this is to us in all our prayers to God! The Urim and Thummim, by which the will of God was made known in doubtful cases, were placed in this breastplate. Urim and Thummim signify light and integrity. There are many guesses as to what these were; the most likely opinion seems to be that they were the twelve precious stones in the high priest’s breastplate. Now, Christ is our Oracle. Through him God makes himself and his mind known to us in these last days; he is the true Light, the faithful Witness, the Truth itself, and from him we receive the Spirit of Truth, who leads into all truth.

The robe of the ephod was under the ephod and reached down to the knees, without sleeves. Aaron must minister in the appointed garments. We must serve the Lord with holy fear, as those who know they deserve to die. A golden plate was fixed on Aaron’s forehead, engraved with “Holiness to the Lord.” Aaron was reminded by this that God is holy and that his priests must be holy, devoted to the Lord. This must appear on their foreheads, in an open declaration of their relationship to God. It must be engraved like the markings of a signet; deep and lasting; not painted so as to be washed off but firm and permanent; such must our holiness to the Lord be. Christ is our High Priest; through him sins are forgiven us and not charged against us. Our persons, our actions, are pleasing to God because of Christ, and no other reason.

The priest’s garments symbolize the righteousness of Christ. If we do not appear before God in that way, we will bear our sin and die. Blessed is he who watches and keeps his garments. And blessed be God that we have a High Priest appointed by God and set apart for his work; equipped for his high office by the glory of his Divine majesty and the beauty of perfect holiness. We are fortunate if, through spiritual understanding of the law, we see that such a High Priest was fitting for us; that we cannot draw near to a holy God or be accepted except through him. There is no light, no wisdom, no perfection but from him; no glory, no beauty but in being like unto him. Let us take encouragement from the power, love, and compassion of our High Priest to approach boldly to the throne of grace so that we may obtain mercy and find help in time of need.
